title: Maribyrnong plan to limit future flood damage
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Local Government Business Maribyrnong plan to limit future flood damage The Age Scott.Symonds 2 May 2026 449 words English AGEE 8 © 2026 Copyright John Fairfax Holdings Limited. With some residents still unable to return home more than three years after the Maribyrnong River flood disaster, the local council has released a plan to help locals protect themselves against the next, inevitable deluge. Maribyrnong City Council is working with Monash University and the community to develop strategies to minimise damage and danger during future flood alerts. The Draft Maribyrnong Community Flood Plan includes emergency actions for residents that include sandbagging toilets and drains, switching off electricity and gas, securing external doors and moving cars to higher ground. The plan stresses the need for community coordination, and includes a network of resident leaders to be set up by December. According to the State Emergency Service, the river has flooded 27 times since 1871, with large floods every 10-20 years. Experts warn the floods will become worse due to urban development and climate change. "They are getting higher, bigger, quicker and more frequent," says water resource and infrastructure planning professional Bernie O'Kane. More than 250 homes were flooded when the river breached its banks in October 2022, leaving residents to cope with a swathe of structural, economic and psychological destruction. "The insurance and building repair process has been lengthy, with some residents still unable to return home three years on," the plan's introduction says. "The mental health and wellbeing impacts of the flood have been widely reported, including through submissions to the Legislative Council Environment and Planning Committee Inquiry into the 2022 Flood. "The community saw significant challenges, from insurance affordability, availability, poor claims handing, insufficient cash settlements and lengthy processes. Residents also describe challenges navigating available supports and system accessibility more broadly." The floods, and subsequent revised flood mapping, left some homes unsellable and uninsurable, while insurance costs on other homes skyrocketed beyond the owners' ability to pay. Residents of Rivervue Retirement Village in Avondale Heights, which was inundated during the flood, have launched a class action blaming Melbourne Water for shoddy flood modelling and development approvals. Last year, Victorian Ombudsman Marlo Baragwanath recommended they be compensated as the flooding was the result of government failures. "The flooding of Rivervue homes stems from failures by the state," Baragwanath said. "Residents, through no fault of their own, are now left significantly out of pocket and forced to spend their retirement in a flood-prone area." Her report urged broader reforms including better information for residents, planning that accounts for climate change and programs to support residents facing flooding. The Ombudsman's findings followed a series of investigations into the Maribyrnong flood disaster by The Age that uncovered failures in planning decisions and the emergency warning system. summary: Maribyrnong City Council has unveiled a Draft Community Flood Plan to mitigate future flood risks following the 2022 disaster that left many residents displaced. The plan includes emergency preparedness actions and emphasizes community coordination, with a network of resident leaders to be established. Experts warn that climate change and urban development will exacerbate flooding issues in the area.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: General Insurance
key_points: The plan aims to protect residents from future floods and includes emergency actions like sandbagging and securing properties.
Over 250 homes were affected in the 2022 flood, leading to ongoing challenges with insurance and mental health for residents.
The Victorian Ombudsman has called for reforms to improve flood planning and support for affected residents.
relevance: Understanding flood risk management and insurance implications is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals involved in risk assessment and community planning.
